nativeRange.habitat: Coral and rocky reefs in tropical Indo-Pacific waters, with a strong population centred on the Hawaiian Islands (where it is locally known as 'Ula). Shelters by day in caves, crevices, and overhangs in the reef structure and emerges at night to scavenge and hunt across the substrate.

decorPreferences: abundant live rock arranged into caves, crevices, and overhangs for daytime shelter
stable rockwork wedged or epoxied to prevent collapse during burrowing
thick gravel or coarse sand bed where the lobster can excavate retreats
secure post-molt hideaway away from tankmates while the new exoskeleton hardens
tightly-fitting lid — reef lobsters are widely reported to climb rockwork, pumps, and hoses and can escape open-top tanks

diet.notes: Generally considered an easy feeder — a nocturnal scavenger and opportunistic hunter that takes most meaty marine foods: chopped shrimp, mussel, clam, squid, krill, mysis, and sinking pellets or carnivore wafers. Target-feeding with feeding tongs after lights-out is commonly advised to ensure it gets enough food without competing with daytime tankmates. Periodic iodine, calcium, magnesium, and trace-element supplementation are typically recommended to support successful molts.

goodWith: medium and large robust reef fish that sleep above the substrate (tangs, larger wrasses, angelfish)
large mobile invertebrates that can avoid it
most LPS, SPS, soft corals, and zoanthids (when the lobster is well-fed)
established mature reef systems with plenty of rockwork
avoidWith: conspecifics and other reef lobsters — extremely territorial toward each other
small bottom-resting or sleeping fish (gobies, blennies, small wrasses)
ornamental shrimp (cleaner shrimp, peppermint shrimp, sexy shrimp)
small hermit crabs and snails it can crush at night
octopus, mantis shrimp, large triggerfish, and other large crustacean predators
any system recently treated with copper-based medications

summary: Vivid 10–14 cm Indo-Pacific reef lobster — bright red to red-orange overall with white spots and tufts of bristles along the carapace and claws. Strictly nocturnal and reclusive, it shelters in rockwork caves by day and forages at night; generally considered reef safe with caution.
careNotes: A reclusive, strictly nocturnal lobster best appreciated as a cryptic reef-tank inhabitant rather than a centerpiece animal — most of the day it stays wedged into a cave or crevice and emerges only after lights-out. The slightly larger, more widely-traded cousin of the Purple Reef Lobster (Enoplometopus debelius) and very similar in care. Setup should provide abundant live rock arranged into deep caves and overhangs, plus a coarse gravel or sand bed it can excavate; rockwork should be wedged, epoxied, or otherwise stabilised because the lobster is known to undermine loose aquascapes. Generally considered reef safe with caution: it will largely ignore healthy, alert fish but has been reported to grab small fish (gobies, blennies, sleeping wrasses) that rest on the substrate at night, and it will pick off ornamental shrimp, small hermits, and snails — especially if underfed. Strictly one specimen (or, rarely, a confirmed mated pair) per tank; conspecifics fight to the death. Like all marine crustaceans, it is highly intolerant of copper-based medications and any history of copper dosing in the system should be regarded as disqualifying. A tightly-fitting lid is commonly advised: reef lobsters are widely reported to climb pumps, hoses, and rockwork and have escaped open-top tanks. Note on taxonomy: the correct family is Enoplometopidae (reef lobsters) — several retailer sources, including LiveAquaria, incorrectly list this species under Nephropidae (the family containing true clawed lobsters such as the American lobster); reef lobsters were split into their own family in the late twentieth century. Source ranges varied modestly: temperature 22–27°C across sources (averaged to 23–26°C); pH 8.0–8.5 (written as 8.1–8.4); specific gravity 1.023–1.025; max size 12–15 cm (written as 10–14 cm). Reported minimum tank sizes ranged from 12 to 50 US gallons; written here as 200 L (~50 gal) to give the animal adequate rockwork footprint and a separate post-molt retreat — consistent with the larger end of source recommendations and with the published full adult size.
breedingNotes: Breeding is rarely reported in home aquaria. Sexes are separate; females carry the fertilised eggs attached to the pleopods until hatching, releasing planktonic larvae that pass through several long-lived pelagic zoeal stages before settlement. Captive rearing is widely regarded as impractical without a dedicated larval-culture setup, and aquarium specimens are essentially all wild-caught from the Indo-Pacific (most often from Hawaiian fisheries).

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied primary source root resolved via search to the current Red Hawaiian Reef Lobster product page. Confirmed temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, max size ~5 in (13 cm), omnivore, semi-aggressive, reef-safe with caution, copper-sensitive, nocturnal, one-specimen-per-tank rule. Note: LiveAquaria states family Nephropidae — this is a common retailer error; the correct family is Enoplometopidae per Wikipedia and modern decapod taxonomy.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Designated secondary. Confirmed Hawaiian origin, ~5 in (13 cm) max size, omnivore, semi-aggressive, reef-safe, beginner care level. Stated minimum tank size 50 US gal (~190 L) — used to anchor the cautious 200 L floor adopted here. Did not provide water-parameter ranges.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12), confirmed nocturnal habit, copper sensitivity, molting supplementation needs (calcium/magnesium/iodine/trace elements), and one-specimen-per-tank rule. Lists 6 in (15 cm) max size — used to widen the size range. States 12 gal minimum tank which was rejected as an obvious under-statement for an adult-size animal in this family. Also lists family Nephropidae — same retailer error.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (15 cm / 6 in), broader temperature range (24–27°C), pH 8.0–8.5, SG 1.023–1.025, 100 L (26 gal) minimum tank, escape-artist habit, semi-aggressive territoriality, and confirmation of family Enoplometopidae (matches Wikipedia). Used to widen averaged ranges.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Enoplometopus occidentalis)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy and wild biology: family Enoplometopidae (NOT Nephropidae as some retailers list), order Decapoda; Indo-West Pacific distribution centred on Hawaii but extending to the eastern coast of Africa, Indonesia, Philippines, and the Indian Ocean; depth range 3–30 m on coral and rocky reefs; max ~14 cm (5.5 in) total length; originally described as Nephrops occidentalis by Randall 1840. IUCN Least Concern. Not used for husbandry parameters.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Healthy adults are generally reported to molt every one to two months; juveniles molt more frequently. The lobster typically retreats into a cave or burrow to molt and is highly vulnerable until the new exoskeleton hardens — provide a secure post-molt hideaway and resist removing the shed exoskeleton, which can be left in the tank as a calcium and chitin source. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium, and iodine levels are commonly cited as supporting successful molts; sudden parameter swings are the most-reported cause of failed molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Females carry fertilised eggs on the pleopods until hatching, releasing long-lived planktonic zoeae that are widely considered impractical to rear in home aquaria. Aquarium specimens are essentially all wild-caught, predominantly from Hawaii.
